title: Why the trigger of being asked to relax makes everything worse
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
You know the feeling 
 Someone tells you to relax. Maybe it's a partner. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a doctor or a yoga teacher. And something in you just....
metaDescription:
You know the feeling 
 Someone tells you to relax. Maybe it's a partner. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a doctor or a yoga teacher. And something in you...
htmlContent:
You know the feeling

Someone tells you to relax. Maybe it's a partner. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a doctor or a yoga teacher. And something in you just... tightens. Hardens. Gets angry. Or maybe you feel a wave of shame wash over you because you can't seem to do the simple thing they're asking. Here's the thing: that reaction isn't a character flaw. It's your nervous system responding to a threat. And the threat is real, even if nobody else can see it.

I've been there. Sitting in a meditation class, legs crossed, eyes closed, and the instructor says "just let go." And my jaw clenches. My shoulders go up toward my ears. My mind starts racing with all the reasons I can't let go. I'm failing at relaxing. I'm doing it wrong. I'm broken. Stay with me here, because this is where it gets interesting. The very act of being told to relax activates the exact opposite response in a traumatized nervous system. It's not your fault. It's science.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would explain this as a neuroception of danger. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and threat, below the level of conscious awareness. When someone tells you to relax, especially if that person has authority over you or if relaxation has been weaponized against you in the past, your system detects a mismatch. The command to relax feels like a demand. And demands, for many of us, are not safe.

The command to calm down

Think about the last time someone told you to calm down. Did it work? Probably not. For most of us, it does the opposite. It's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. The instruction doesn't address the underlying injury. It just adds shame on top of pain.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your mind. It's a physiological state. When your nervous system is stuck in a survival response - fight, flight, freeze, or fawn - being told to relax is like being told to stop breathing. You can't just will yourself out of a survival state. Your body has to feel safe enough to come back to rest on its own terms.

I remember a therapist once told me to "just breathe deeply" during a panic attack. I wanted to scream at her. I was already breathing. I was hyperventilating. My body was doing exactly what it needed to do to survive. Her instruction felt like a criticism. Like I was doing something wrong by being in a state I couldn't control. It took me years to understand that my reaction wasn't resistance. It was protection.

Here's what I wish someone had told me: your nervous system has good reasons for doing what it does. It's not broken. It's doing its job. The problem isn't that you can't relax. The problem is that the environment or the people around you haven't given your system the safety signals it needs to shift states.

Why being asked to relax triggers the trauma response

If you grew up in a home where your emotions were dismissed, controlled, or punished, the command to relax carries a hidden message. It says: your current state is unacceptable. You need to change it to be acceptable to me. For someone with a history of emotional neglect or invalidation, this is a direct hit to the nervous system.

Janina Fisher, who works extensively with trauma and the structural dissociation model, describes how parts of us get activated by these kinds of triggers. There's the part that wants to comply, the part that feels shame for not being able to comply, and the part that's fiercely guarding against any kind of control. All of them are trying to protect you. But they're pulling in different directions, and that internal conflict feels like chaos.

I've worked with clients who describe feeling trapped when someone tells them to relax. They want to comply. They want to feel peaceful. But their body says no. And then they feel guilty for not being able to do something that seems so simple for everyone else. The shame spiral is real, and it's exhausting.

One book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why being told what to feel - even if it's "just relax" - can feel so violating. When your emotional reality was never honored, any attempt to control your internal state by someone else feels like a repetition of that original wound.

Wild, right? The thing that's supposed to help you actually makes things worse. But here's the truth: it's not relaxation that's the problem. It's the demand. It's the lack of choice. It's the feeling that your body isn't your own.

What actually helps when you can't relax

So what do you do when you're stuck in that place where every suggestion to relax makes you more tense? The answer is counterintuitive. You stop trying to relax. You stop trying to change your state at all. You let your nervous system be exactly where it is, without judgment.

This is hard. Really hard. Because we've been told our whole lives that we need to fix ourselves. That being anxious or tense or hypervigilant is a problem to be solved. But what if it's not a problem? What if it's just information? What if your nervous system is telling you something important about your environment or your history?

I've found that the most effective approach is to work with the body, not against it. Instead of trying to force relaxation, I focus on noticing. What does tension feel like in my body right now? Where is it? What shape does it take? Does it have a color or a texture? This isn't about making it go away. It's about building a relationship with your own experience.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the nervous system in a gentle way, without the demand to "calm down." It's not a magic bullet, but for some people it takes the edge off enough to actually feel what's happening in their body without the panic.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). It explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, and how your nervous system might be carrying responses that aren't even yours. That knowledge alone can be liberating. It's not that you're broken. It's that you're carrying something heavy that was handed to you.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It names the experience of growing up in a home where your emotional needs weren't met, and how that shows up in your adult body. When you understand the source of the tension, the tension itself becomes less frightening.

Reclaiming your right to not be okay

Here's where I'm going to get direct with you. The wellness industry has sold us a lie. It's told us that we should always be calm, centered, and peaceful. That if we just meditate enough, do enough yoga, breathe enough, we'll reach some state of permanent bliss. That's not how human beings work. That's not how trauma works. And that expectation just adds another layer of shame on top of an already burdened nervous system.

You have the right to be tense. You have the right to be angry. You have the right to be anxious. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's trying to keep you alive. And until your body truly believes it's safe, it's not going to let go of those survival strategies just because someone tells you to relax.

The path forward isn't about forcing relaxation. It's about creating conditions where your nervous system can choose to relax on its own. That means safety. That means choice. That means patience. And that means honoring the wisdom of your body, even when it's doing something that doesn't make sense to your conscious mind.

I still struggle with this. Someone tells me to relax and I feel that familiar clench in my chest. But now I know what it is. I know it's not a failure. It's a signal. And instead of fighting it, I say to myself: "Okay. I see you. You don't have to change. You're allowed to be exactly where you are." And sometimes, when I really mean it, my body softens on its own.

Not always. Not perfectly. But sometimes. And that's enough for now.

  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A gentle supplement that supports nervous system regulation
